elements.sent-2411-001: Tight and essentially tight modules generalize weakly injective modules.

elements.sent-2411-004: In this note, we investigate cases when those two notions are the same.

elements.sent-2411-007: Weakly injective modules were introduced by Jain and Lopez \cite{jain1990rings as a generalization of injective modules in their study of CEP rings, which are rings whose cyclics are essentially embeddable in a projective module.}
elements.sent-2411-008: They proved that a ring $R$ is CEP if and only if it is artinian and every indecomposable projective module is uniform and weakly $R-$injective.
elements.sent-2411-009: Later, Golan and Lopez \cite{golan1991qi further generalized weak injectivity into what they coined as tightness.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Saleh \cite{saleh1999note proved a more general result than Jain and Lopez's characterization of CEP rings replacing weak injectivity by tightness.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Recently, Koşan, Quynh and Serap \cite{kocsan2017weakly introduced another generalization to weak injectivity, namely essential tightness.}
elements.sent-2411-012: They proved a characterization of CEP ring similar to Saleh's using essential tightness.
elements.sent-2411-013: It is noted in \cite{kocsan2017weakly that not every tight module is essentially tight.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In this short note, we provide different cases when those notions are actually equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-015: To do this, we review, in section 2, fundamental results on weak injectivity, tightness and essential tightness from literature, covering fundamentals of q.f.d and semiprime Goldie rings.
elements.sent-2411-016: The equivalence between the tightness and essential tightness is discussed in section 3.
elements.sent-2411-017: The main theorem in this regard is Theorem 3.11.
elements.sent-2411-018: Both tightness and essentially tight modules generalize weak injectivity by imposing or removing essentiality on both embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-019: Imposing it on only one of embeddings provides us with yet two generalizations of weak injectivity, one is a special case of both tight and essentially tight and the other is a generalization of both.
elements.sent-2411-020: In section 4, we define those and prove some results on them focusing on CEP ring characterizations.
elements.sent-2411-021: It turns out that most results are similar to weakly injective, tight and essentially tight modules.

elements.sent-2411-023: A module $M$ is called weakly $N-$injective if for any $\sigma: N \to E(M)$, there exists a submodule $X$ of $E(M)$ such that $\sigma(N) \subset X \simeq M$.
elements.sent-2411-024: On the other hand, $M$ is said to be (essentially) $N-$tight, if for every submodule $K$ of $N$, whenever $N/K$ (essentially) embeds into $E(M)$, then $N/K$ (essentially) embeds into $M$.
elements.sent-2411-025: When $M$ is (essentially) $N-$tight for every finitely generated module $N$, then $M$ is said to be (essentially) tight.
elements.sent-2411-026: Hence, Any weakly injective module is both tight and essentially tight.

elements.sent-2411-032: A ring $R$ is said to be q.f.d if every cyclic module has a finitely generated, possibly trivial, socle.
elements.sent-2411-033: Hence a ring is q.f.d if every cyclic module has a finite Goldie dimension.
elements.sent-2411-034: One important class of q.f.d rings is the class of noetherian rings.
elements.sent-2411-035: One important result over q.f.d is that of Lopez \cite[Theorem 3.1]{lopez1992rings which establishes an equivalence between weak injectivity and tightness over q.f.d rings.}
